coredata.dc:title: Z-scheme CuBi2O4/carbon dots nanoflowers heterojunction to enhance solar photocatalytic sterilization

coredata.dc:description: Here, CuBi2O4/carbon dots (CBO-CDs) were prepared to reveal their high-performance of solar photocatalytic sterilization. Firstly, CuBi2O4 nanoflower assembled by nanotubes were synthesized via a hydrothermal strategy through the regulation of pH condition. The primary nanotubes shorten the distance for charge transfer, and the novel hierarchical structure also provides abundant active sites to enrich substrate. After the loading of CDs, the composites enhance light harvesting across the entire spectrum. Moreover, they possess Z-scheme mechanism endowing efficient charge separation and high redox potential. The photogenerated electrons can capture the dissolved O2 to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can induce cellular inactivation by cleaving cell membrane and degrading cytogenetic constituents. The notable solar photocatalytic ability not only inactivate E. coli (96.88 ± 1.50 %) and S. aureus (99.96 ± 1.41 %) but also can damage their biofilms (above 91.70 ± 2.54 %) efficiently. Moreover, the practical complex lake sample was also investigated to reveal great bacteriostatic (97.65 ± 1.30 %) and methylene blue degradation (99.91 ± 1.40 %) ability, simultaneously. Additionally, CBO-CDs also can disinfect mask under irradiation, contributing to the development of self-cleaning mask. Given their broad-spectrum antimicrobial, novel biocompatibility, and solar-activation, CBO-CDs possess great practical application potential for sterilization.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, CuBi2O4/carbon dots (CBO-CDs) were prepared to reveal their high-performance of solar photocatalytic sterilization. Firstly, CuBi2O4 nanoflower assembled by nanotubes were synthesized via a hydrothermal strategy through the regulation of pH condition. The primary nanotubes shorten the distance for charge transfer, and the novel hierarchical structure also provides abundant active sites to enrich substrate. After the loading of CDs, the composites enhance light harvesting across the entire spectrum. Moreover, they possess Z-scheme mechanism endowing efficient charge separation and high redox potential. The photogenerated electrons can capture the dissolved O2 to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can induce cellular inactivation by cleaving cell membrane and degrading cytogenetic constituents. The notable solar photocatalytic ability not only inactivate E. coli (96.88 ± 1.50 %) and S. aureus (99.96 ± 1.41 %) but also can damage their biofilms (above 91.70 ± 2.54 %) efficiently. Moreover, the practical complex lake sample was also investigated to reveal great bacteriostatic (97.65 ± 1.30 %) and methylene blue degradation (99.91 ± 1.40 %) ability, simultaneously. Additionally, CBO-CDs also can disinfect mask under irradiation, contributing to the development of self-cleaning mask. Given their broad-spectrum antimicrobial, novel biocompatibility, and solar-activation, CBO-CDs possess great practical application potential for sterilization.

ce:section.ce:para: Based on the data from World Health Organization, the consumption of water contaminated with microbes results in approximately 829,000 deaths annually [1–3]. An efficient strategy is desired to handle the big problem. Currently, various efforts have been developed for water disinfection, such as: chemical reagents (ozone, chlorine, hydrogen peroxide, and alcohol) [4–6], ultraviolet irradiation (UV-A, UV-B and UV-C) [7–9], and antibiotics [10]. However, several drawbacks also can't be ignored, including high operational costs, the formation of mutagenic and carcinogenic side-products, and the development of drug-resistant bacteria and even superbugs [11,12]. Thus, the development of effective, green, low-cost, safe sterilization techniques is significant to treat drinking water.

Considering the advantages of high-efficiency, low cost, and environmental benignity, solar photocatalysis possesses the great potential on advanced for photocatalytic disinfection [13–15]. Under sunlight irradiation, a photocatalyst can be excited to form electrons (e−) and holes (h+), which undergo the following redox reactions to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and to induce cellular inactivation by oxidative damage. That means the photocatalytic disinfection also is a type of oxidation processes (AOPs). Beside the above advantages, the AOP can't cause drug resistance that is pivotal to antibacterial application. Currently, various bismuth-based compounds (BiVO4, Bi2MoO6, Bi2O3) have been widely applied in photocatalysis [16–18]. Owing to the suitable band gap (1.5–1.8 eV), CuBi2O4 can fully utilize of solar energy, which is associated with good photocatalytic activity and excellent photostability to grant it various applications, including H2 evolution [19,20], organic contamination degradation [21], H2O2 production [22]. However, single CuBi2O4 exhibits dissatisfactory photocatalytic activity due to the quick recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes. Comparison with the heteroatom doping, the construction of heterostructure is thought to be more resultful to restrict the spatial distribution of electrons and holes into different constituents. For example, Mohammad et al. reported the construction of an iron oxide/CdS heterojunction. The relative band alignment of the constituent materials promotes a spatial separation of the electron and hole under photoexcitation, granting a highly desirable property for photocatalytic applications [23]. Priyanka et al. reported AgXSe2-TiO2 (X = In, Bi) nano-heterojunction to exhibit a p-n nano-heterojunction nature. The heterojunctions facilitated effective charge separation by delaying the recombination of charge carriers, thereby contributing to enhanced photocatalytic performance [24]. Amony these heterostructure, Z-Scheme possess great potential on the construct of photocatalyst because it can not only restrict the recombination of photo-generated charge but also maintain high redox ability [25]. To further make sure the charge separation, the abundant interfaces and short transfer route are crucial for charge transfer. Therefore, the high surface area and sufficient contact of the two components also can contribute to charge separation.

Besides, the great utilization of solar also is crucial for photocatalytic efficiency. It is known that proportion of infrared light in the whole in infrared region, it can obtain giant energy from solar. Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great attention as photocatalysts on account of their unique physical and chemical properties [26,27]. Owing to the concomitant sp2 and sp3 carbon segments, CDs always show broad light harvest behavior derived from the spatial overlap of energy levels [28], which makes CDs as significant component or cocatalyst in solar-driven photocatalysis [29]. In addition, nitrogen-doped CDs material can effectively induce the delocalization of charges to help charge separation and also can increase the conductivity owing to the injection of lone pair electrons from N atom [30].

In this work, CuBi2O4-CDs photocatalyst was prepared to reveal the great photocatalytic sterilization capacity. Firstly, CuBi2O4 nanomaterials were synthesized via hydrothermal method. By controlling the pH condition, the nanoflower was obtained, which was assembled by many nanotubes. Then, N-CDs solution was synthesized using methylene blue and ethylenediamine as precursor. After another hydrothermal treatment, CuBi2O4-CDs heterostructure was obtained, which revealed the novel Z-scheme feature. The primary nanotube structure grants the short distance for charge transfer. Moreover, the hierarchical structure is conductive to the adsorption toward substrate, contributing to the enrichment of the substances for catalytic reaction. CuBi2O4-CDs also enhance light harvest even in infrared region owing to the assembly of CDs. Under stimulated sunlight, CuBi2O4-CDs reveal outstanding performance in MB degradation and disinfection abilities. The disinfection mechanism also was investigated, revealing that ROS can oxidize and cleave cell membranes to induce the leakage of cytogenetic material for cellular inactivation. Several practical water and mask models also were carried out to discover the broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity.

All chemical reagents used were of analytical grade and were employed directly without further purification. Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3
5H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), albumin from bovine serum and methylene blue (MB) were purchased from Aladdin Co. Ltd. Cupric nitrate (Cu(NO)2
3H2O) was obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co. Ltd. Nitric acid (HNO3) was purchased from Harbin Institute of Technology Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) was purchased from Tianjin Kemio Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Ethylenediamine (C2H8N2) was obtained from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Peptone, agar powder, and yeast extract powder were purchased from Beijing Aoboxing Bio-tech Co. Ltd. Iodonitrotetrazolium chloride, β-Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide hydrate and phenazinemethosulfate were obtained from Innochem. Coomassie Brilliant Blue G-250 was purchased from Beijing Biotopped. Calcein AM was purchased from Alfa Aesar.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected by Hitachi H-8100 at an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer with Cu Ka radiation (40 kV, 20 mA). SU-70 UHR field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was employed to discover the sample morphology. UV–Vis spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV2550 spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were surveyed by HORIBA FL-3. The relative photocatalytic experiments were carried out using Xenon Lamp (HSF–F300, Beijing Newbit Technology Co., Ltd.) with a filter (AM1.5G). The incident light exhibits a power density of 0.101 W cm−2 (LP100, Changchun New Industry Photoelectric Technology Co., Ltd.).

0.3 g of methylene blue and 500 μL of ethylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. Next, 10 mL of sulfuric acid was added to the forward mixed solution and stirred for 30 min. Then, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL of Teflon autoclave heated at 200 °C for 24 h. After cooled to room temperature, the solution was neutralized to 7 using NaOH solution. The solution was centrifuged at 3000 rpm and the supernatant was dialyzed for subsequent use.

CuBi2O4 was synthesized by hydrothermal method. Typically, 0.7 mmol of Bi(NO3)3
5H2O was dissolved in 1 mL of concentrated HNO3 solution. And 1.4 mmol of CuSO4
5H2O was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water, respectively. Then, CuSO4 solution was slowly added dropwise to Bi(NO3)3 solution under continuous stirring. Next, 14 mL of NaOH solution (0.6 M) was slowly added above mixture and stirred for 1 h. After that, the solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon lined autoclave heated at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the sample was washed with deionized water and dried at 70 °C for 12 h to obtain CuBi2O4 (CBO). To investigate the acidity influence, the volume of nitric acid is 0.8, 0.95, 1, and 1.1 mL to obtain CBO-1, CBO-2, CBO-3, and CBO-4, respectively.

0.1 g of CBO-3 and 7 mL of CDs solution were dissolved in 3 mL of deionized water. Then, the mixture was transferred to 50 mL Teflon autoclave heated at 120 °C for 12 h. After that, the sample was washed with deionized water and dried at 70 °C for 12 h to obtain a black powder product, named CBO-CDs. With different volumes of CDs solution, the samples were denoted as CBO-CDs (5 mL) and CBO-CDs (9 mL), respectively.

The photocatalytic degradation of MB was assessed using 300 W Xe-lamp with AM1.5G cutoff filter. Generally, 25 mg of catalyst was distributed into 50 mL of MB solution (10 mg/L) and placed in dark for 30 min to reach the adsorption equilibrium. During irradiation, 3 mL of sample solution was taken out and centrifugated to collect supernatant at given time intervals. The absorbance at 665 nm was adopted to evaluate MB concentration. In ROS scavenging experiments, p-benzoquinone (BQ, 2 mM), ethylenediamine tetra acetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na, 2 mM), and silver nitrate (AgNO3, 2 mM) were employed as scavengers. The degradation process was the same as described above.

To assess the photocatalytic stability, the degradation experiment was repeated four times in a cyclic manner. After each experiment, the catalyst was collected by centrifugation and then washed three times with deionized water before being dried in an oven. The recycled catalyst was reused in the subsequent MB degradation experiment, and this process was repeated for four cycles. If the recycled sample amount is lower than 25 mg, certain new sample (about 5–7 mg) were added to make sure the total amount of catalyst is the same for each cycle.

Photoelectrochemical properties were determined using sodium sulfate solution (0.5 M) as electrolyte from electrochemical workstation (CHI-660E, Shanghai Chenhua, China). Pt and Ag/AgCl were used as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The working electrode was obtained by coating the catalyst solution onto an ITO glass and vacuum drying. Photocurrent response experiments were performed under a 300 W xenon lamp with AM 1.5G.

Wild-type E. coli and S. aureus (ATCC 25922 and ATCC 43300, Shanghai Luwei Technology Co., Ltd) were employed for disinfection experiments. 1.3 mg of photocatalyst was added in 10 mL of E. coli suspension. Then, the suspension was irradiated by 300 W Xe lamp (AM 1.5G). At 0, 4, and 8 min, 100 μL of bacterial solution was taken out and coated on LB solid medium, incubated at 37 °C for 24 h for colony-forming units (CFU) counting. In S. aureus experiment, 2.4 mg of photocatalyst was adopted and the other procedure is the same as above. Before experiment, all glass apparatuses were autoclaved at 121 °C for 25 min. The antibacterial rate was calculated using following Eq. (1):
Eq. 1

Nt: the number of single colonies at different times.
N0: the number of single colonies of control group.

The bacterial suspensions, which were treated by different conditions, were centrifuged (8000 rpm and 5 min) to collect bacteria. Then, they were fixed with glutaraldehyde (2.5 %) overnight at 4 °C and followed by washing with PBS twice. After the dehydration in sequential ethanol solutions (10, 30, 50, 70, 90, and 100 vol %) for 15 min each time, the bacteria were analyzed by using SEM.

Cell culture: human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) were cultured in RPMI 1640 solution including 10 % (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS) and penicillin/streptomycin (100 U/mL/100 mg/mL) in a humidified 5 % CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C.

MTT assay: cells were seeded into 96-well plates at 8 × 103 cells per well overnight to make sure attachment and then they were incubated with CBO-CDs media for 24 h. Afterwards, these media were removed and cells were incubated with fresh media containing MTT (0.5 mg/mL) for another 4 h. The final medium was then replaced with 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) per well and the absorbance was collected from microplate reader (WD-2102A) at 492 nm. The cytotoxicity was calculated as the percentage of cell viability compared to untreated group.

For AM staining: HUVEC cells were incubated with CBO-CDs (100 μg/mL, 1 mL) for 24 h, then cells were washed by PBS (pH = 7.4) three times. The cells were stained with Calcein-AM (AM) for 30 min and washed by PBS for three times. The fluorescence imaging was carried out on Leica DFC450C Microsystems.

In order to further investigate the antibacterial properties, the anti-biofilms activity also was evaluated. S. aureus/E. coli (1 × 108 CFU/mL, 1 mL) was seeded in a 96-well plate incubated for 3 days at 37 °C for mature biofilm formation. Of note, medium was replaced daily. Add the photocatalyst solution (PBS = 7.4) to 96 well plate. Subsequent to this, the biofilm samples underwent either light treatment (AM 1.5G, 10 min) or were incubated in an undisturbed state of silence. The biofilms were then washed with PBS. For further study, the residual biofilms were dyed with crystal violet. To dissolve the dye deposited on the biofilms for quantification, a 33 % acetic acid solution was used. For quantitative measurement, eluting the dye adsorbed on the biofilms with an ethanol solution was used and measuring the OD at 570 nm.

To survey the ROS independent oxidative stress, in vitro GSH oxidation was evaluated using Ellman's assay [31]. Prepare material dispersions at concentrations of 4 mg/mL, with the solvent being PBS solution at pH = 7.4. GSH solution (0.8 mM) was added into sample solution (volume ratio of 2:1), incubated at 37 °C for 40 min, then centrifuge. Take 100 μL of the supernatant, add 800 μL of PBS pH 7.4 and 10 μL of DTNB solution (4 mg/mL, PBS 7.4), incubate at 37 °C for 10 min, then centrifuge and take the supernatant for UV absorption testing. Measurements of the thiol content with all samples were carried out at 412 nm using AMR-100 (Hangzhou Allsheng Instruments Co., Ltd.). Pure GSH solution was used as negative control and GSH solution treated with H2O2 (1 mM) as positive control. The loss of GSH was expressed using Eq. (2).

The Bradford assay was utilized to quantitatively assess the leakage of intracellular proteins from bacterial cells [32]. The materials were incubated with bacterial cells (106 CFU mL−1) at 37 °C for 6 h in an incubator shaker. Subsequently, 1 mL of the treated bacterial cultures from each test vial were withdrawn and centrifuged at 1600 rpm for 30 min at 4 °C. Finally, 5 equivalent of Bradford reagent (4.7 % w/v ethanol, 0.01 % w/v Coomassie Brilliant Blue G-250, and 8.5 % w/v phosphoric acid) was added to the 1 equivalent of collected supernatants, and the absorbance at 595 nm was measured to determine the protein concentration.

The LDH test was carried out to evaluate cell membrane integrity, based on the principle that the cytosolic enzyme LDH can release into the culture medium because cells die can lead in plasma membrane damage. Frist, the LDH reagent was prepared by adding 50 μL each of PIN (1.175 mM PMS, 2.610 mM INT, 5.185 mM NAD) solution, 50 mM lithium lactate, and 200 mM TRIS (pH 8). Bacterial (∼106 CFU mL−1) were incubated with samples and irradiation in an incubator shaker for 6 h at 37 °C. After the required duration, the samples were centrifuged for 5 min at 1200 rpm. In a 96-well plate, 180 μL of LDH reagent was added to each well containing 60 μL of supernatant and incubated for 30 min at room temperature in dark. Following incubation, the optical density of each well was recorded at 490 nm. 0.1 % Triton X-100 and 0.85 % saline suspensions were adopted as positive and negative controls, respectively. The percentage of LDH release was calculated using the OD490 values according to Eq. (3). The LDH activity of the positive control was considered as 100 %, and the activity of all other test samples was calculated relative to this value [33].

Firstly, CuBi2O4 composites were synthesized by a hydrothermal process, in which the effect of HNO3 concentration on morphology was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The spherical structure with the diameter of approximately 2.5 μm was formed when the concentration of HNO3 is 0.32 M, and the sphere was assembled by many nanoparticles (CBO-1, Fig. 1 A). From Fig. 1B, as the concentration of HNO3 increases to 0.38 M, the external shape transformed into nanorods approximately 1 μm in length and 150 nm in diameter (CBO-2). As shown in Fig. 1C, the concentration of HNO3 increased to 0.4 M (CBO-3) to produce nanoflowers (3.5 μm), which was assembled from many nanotubes about 100 nm in diameter. The magnified image further revealed a square cross-section and square holes approximately 30 nm in size (Fig. 1D). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images further testified the hollow nanotube (Fig. 1E). Upon further increasing the concentration of HNO3 to 0.44 M, the morphology of CBO became disordered (CBO-4,Fig. 1F). In view of above, the pH condition (concentration of HNO3) plays a pivotal role in the final morphology of CuBi2O4 because it would affect the rate of hydrolysis and polymerization of precursors [34]. As shown in Fig. S1, The N2 adsorption-desorption analysis was carried out. As expected, the porous structure endows CBO-3 (11.23 m2/g) with the highest surface area than CBO-1 (5.20 m2/g) and CBO-2 (5.93 m2/g). The great surface area facilitates the enrichment of organic molecules on to the surface of catalyst for degradation. As shown in Fig. 1G, the HRTEM image revealed the interplanar spacing about 0.26 nm attributing to (130) plane of monoclinic CuBi2O4. And the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern further reveals the monocrystal structure for nanotube (Fig. 1H). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns showed the typical diffraction peaks for monoclinic CuBi2O4 (JCPDS 42–0334) in CBO-1, CBO-2, and CBO-3 (Fig. 1I). However, the diffraction peaks of Bi2O2CO3 and CuO can be found from CBO-4, indicating the present impure phase under excessively acidic conditions.

In addition, CuBi2O4 (CB-3) was combined with CDs to obtain the CBO-CDs heterostructure. Scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM)-energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) elemental mapping images display the uniform distribution of Bi, Cu, O, and N elements in the composite (Fig. 2 A and B). CBO-CDs also exhibits the featured diffraction peaks of monoclinic CuBi2O4 (JCPDS 42–0334) and no new diffraction peak demonstrates the low content and crystalline structure of CDs (Fig. 2C). FT-IR spectroscopy was carried out to analyze the chemical information. Compared with pure CBO, the new emerging vibrational peaks at 1029, 1245, and 1474 cm−1 in CBO-CDs are attributed to C–O, C–C and CO groups from CDs as shown in Fig. 2D. To further study the component and valence, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed. From Fig. S2, the existing Cu, Bi, O, and C elements can be found from the survey scan of CBO and CBO-CDs. The C signal in CBO is derived from the organic residuals, and the enhanced C band in CBO-CDs can be attributed to the CDs component. From Fig. 2E, the Cu 2p spectrum could be deconvoluted into five peaks. The two representative peaks at 935.43 and 955.30 eV with a spin-energy separation of almost 20 eV are indexed to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, which accompany with three shake-up satellite peaks at 941.22, 943.58 and 962.40 eV, demonstrating the present Cu2+ [35,36]. As shown in Fig. 2F, the O 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks. The strongest peak at 529.76 eV is the feature of O2− (lattice oxygen), and the band at 531.22 eV is originated from O–H. The peak at 532.78 eV indicates the present C–O derived from CDs [37]. As shown in Fig. 2G, the C 1s spectrum shows the peaks at 284.85, 285.79, and 288.14 eV, indicating C–C/CC, C–O and CO bonds from CDS, respectively. Based on the spectrum analysis, CDs has successfully loaded on CuBi2O4. From Fig. 2H, the peaks at 158.84 and 164.16 eV corresponded to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of Bi3+ in CBO-CDs, respectively [38]. Zeta potential also was recorded to survey the modification. From Fig. 2I, the ζ-potential of CBO is −1.81 ± 0.19 mV derived from the negative surface –OH. The introduction of negative CDs (−6.98 ± 0.23 mV) further decreases surface charge to −2.95 ± 0.59 mV (CBO-CDs).

The photocatalytic degradation of MB solution (10 mg/L) was conducted as a model reaction to investigate the degradation ability for organic contamination. In the dark adsorption-desorption process (20 min), the adsorption capacities of CBO-CDs (7 mL), CBO-CDs (5 mL), and CBO-CDs (9 mL) are 33.01 %, 14.83 %, and 15.75 % respectively, which are higher than that of CBO (3.74 %) owing to CDs modification. The great adsorption also contributes to the interaction between catalyst and organic molecules, facilitating degradation. Under simulated sunlight, the absorbance of MB solutions further decreased as shown in Fig. 3 A–D, indicating the photodegradation. After 120 min, the degradation rates reached 66.72 %, 74.44 %, 99.53 %, and 58.68 % for CBO and CBO-CDs (5 mL), CBO-CDs (7 mL) and CBO-CDs (9 mL), respectively. In order to further investigate the degradation capacity, the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetic model was adopted to assess the degradation behavior. As shown in Fig. 3E and F, all groups fit the kinetic model excellently. As expected, the degradation rate constants of CBO-CDs (5 mL) (0.0094 min−1),CBO-CDs (7 mL) (0.0147 min−1) and CBO-CDs (9 mL) (0.0079 min−1) were higher than that of pure CBO (0.00656 min−1). Moreover, total organic carbon (TOC) analysis was conducted to further investigate the mineralization. As shown in Fig. 3G, after the photocatalytic degradation (2 h), the removal of organic carbon reaches 90.87 %, indicating the good mineralization ability. To further confirm the photocatalytic degradation performance of CBO-CDs, the colorless oxytetracycline was used and the degradation experiment was carried out as above. As shown in Fig. S3, with irradiation the absorbance of oxytetracycline solution decreases obviously. After 2 h, CBO-CDs group (90.44 %) reveals the higher degradation rate than that of CBO group (65.62 %). Furthermore, the cyclic photocatalytic experiment was conducted to investigate the stability of CBO-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3H, the degradation efficiency still remains about 99.93 % after four cycles of MB photodegradation. There was no significant change in the XRD diffraction peaks of CBO-CDs before and after four cycles, further demonstrating their excellent stability (Fig. 3I). In addition, the ICP analysis further revealed the low leaching of Cu (0.22 %–0.55 %, 134.24–325.91 ppb) and Bi (0.0001 %–0.0016 %, 0.30–3.12 ppb) during the cycle use for 4 times (Table S1). The above results demonstrate that the construction of CBO-CDs heterostructure not only can produce great ROS but also possesses good stability.

To further survey the degradation performance, the effect of initial concentration of MB (10–80 mg/L) on photocatalytic ability was investigated. The curves about the degradation ability (removed dye/catalyst, mg/g) and time were plotted as shown in Fig. 4 A. At MB concentration of 10 mg/L, the degradation capacity is 19.77 mg/g, which further rise to 55.82 mg/g at the high MB concentration (80 mg/L), demonstrating the great decontamination ability. Additionally, the equilibrium adsorption curve of MB in CBO-CDs during the dark reaction was calculated. With increasing dye concentration, the adsorption amount elevates until it reaches the equilibrium. From Fig. 4B, the adsorption isotherm is a typical I curve, which belongs to Langmuir adsorption [39,40]. The equilibrium adsorption data was further analyzed by using Eq. (4) (Langmuir equation).

Fig. 4C shows a good linear relationship between Ce/qe and Ce, further confirming the Langmuir adsorption feature. The q
m is calculated to be 100 (mg/g) and the high adsorption capacity is ascribed to the hierarchical structure with high surface area, which is beneficial for dye enrichment onto the surface of the photocatalyst for degradation. Capture assays were carried out to explore the possible reactive intermediate in photocatalytic process. To study the photocatalytic mechanism of CBO-CDs for MB degradation, capture experiments were performed and the main active components in the reaction system were identified. Silver nitrate (AgNO3), ethylenediamine tetra acetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) and p-benzoquinone (BQ) were selected as scavengers of electrons (e−), holes (h+) and superoxide radicals (·O2 −), respectively [41,42]. The addition of electron scavenger AgNO3 led to a notable decrease in photocatalytic activity, suggesting that photogenerated electrons play a crucial role (Fig. 4D). As anticipated, when EDTA-2Na is added to sacrifice holes, promoting electron availability and photocatalytic ability as well. The introduction of the ·O2 − scavenger BQ significantly reduced the degradation efficiency, underscoring that ·O2 − is the predominant reactive species. In addition, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was utilized to distinguish the generated ·O2 −. The characteristic signals of DMPO-O2 - were detected under simulated sunlight, as shown in Fig. 4E. In the absence of light, no ESR signal was found, further indicating that ·O2 − is generated in a photo-triggered manner. Additionally, the ·O2 − generation also was confirmed by chemical probe experiments with nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) as indicator. As shown in Fig. S5, the typical absorbance of NBT decreases in all groups, indicating ·O2 − production. The CBO-CDs reveal the higher ·O2 − generation that also is consistent with above degradation data.

To understand the structure–function relationship, UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy was employed to evaluate band gap, firstly. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the absorption edge of CBO is near 700 nm [38,43]. Binary CBO-CDs heterostructure exhibits an improved light absorption ability in full spectrum and even NIR region, attributed to the excellent light harvest from CDs [44,45]. The band gaps were calculated using Tauc plots to be 1.52 eV, 1.90 eV and 1.75 eV for CDs, CBO and CBO-CDs, respectively (Fig. 5B). These results indicate that the loading of CDs on CBO is a valid strategy to improve the efficiency of light utilization, which is pivotal to photocatalytic behavior. Moreover, the flat band potentials (EFB) were measured by the collection of Mott–Schottky (M − S) curves to evaluate the Fermi level (Ef). As shown in Fig. 5C–E, all samples exhibit positive slopes of M − S curves, reflecting the n-type feature. EFB is determined at −1.00, −0.47, and −0.62 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl) for CDs, CBO, and CBO-CDs, respectively. It is known that the potential of conduction band (ECB) of an n-type semiconductor is always more negative than Ef by approximately 0.20 eV [46]. The conduction band (ECB) values are calculated to be −1.00 and −0.47 eV (vs. NHE) for CDs and CBO, respectively. And the valence band (EVB) was calculated as 0.52 and 1.43 eV for CDs and CBO, respectively. The band structure of the composite was established as shown in Fig. 5F. Upon constructing the heterostructure, the Ef position of CDs (−0.80 eV) is more negative than that of CBO (−0.27 eV), thus the accumulated electrons on the Ef of CDs would flow to CBO until an equilibrium is achieved (Fig. 5G). This migration can cause the formation of an internal electric field from CDs to CBO. The electron transfer process can be validated using XPS. As present in Fig. 5H and I, the binding energies of Cu 2p and O 2p in CBO-CDs shift to the lower energy orientation after the loading of CDs, further confirming electron transfer from CDs to CBO. Additionally, the Fermi level of CBO-CDs is −0.42 eV, which is more negative than that of pure CBO (−0.27 eV), reflecting the electron injection. Under the influence of the built-in electric field, the energy band of CDs experiences an upward bending, whereas that of CBO undergoes a downward bending. Both CBO and CDs can be excited to produce electrons and holes upon exposure to simulated solar light. Guided by the internal electric field, electrons in the CB of CBO preferentially transfer to the VB of CDs, establishing a characteristic Z-Scheme interface. This configuration not only enhances the separation of electron-hole but also sustains a higher redox potential. Both of them are conducive to enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. From Fig. 5F, the electrons residing on the CB of CDs possess high reduction potential of −1.00 eV, which is more negative than the potential for oxygen reduction E (O2/ . O2 −) = −0.33 eV to enable the capture of O2 for . O2 − production. The EVB of CBO is just 1.43 eV, which is insufficient to oxidize H2O into . OH directly (EH2O/.OH = 2.30 eV). Therefore, the photocatalytic activity enhance obviously as the holes are sacrificed by EDTA-2Na, which contributes to the accumulation of electrons as shown in Fig. 4D.

In addition, the efficient charge separation and transfer were further investigated using electrochemical methods. As shown in Fig. 6 A, both CBO-CDs and CBO exhibit light-triggered current responses. The transient photocurrent of CBO-CDs is stronger than that of CBO, indicating the presence of more free charges. Furthermore, electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) was also collected to learn the transfer of interface charge. As exhibited in Fig. 6B, the smaller semicircular diameter in CBO-CDs group demonstrates the lower resistance of charge transfer and the quicker mobility of interfacial-charge in comparison with CBO. According to the equivalent circuit model, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of CBO (31.22 Ω) is higher than CBO-CDs (4.79 Ω), indicating the free interfacial charge transfer in CBO-CDs. Besides, photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was measured to further investigate the charge-transfer dynamics. The intensity of PL emission peak of CBO-CDs is quenched compared to that of pure CBO counterpart, indicating that the charge recombination is inhibited (Fig. 6C). These analyses demonstrate that the novel Z-Scheme CBO-CDs heterojunction can synergistically direct the transfer and separation of electrons, effectively suppress the recombination of photogenerated charges, and thereby significantly enhance photocatalytic activity.

The photothermal performance of these samples was also surveyed. As shown in Fig. 6D, the temperature of CBO and CBO-CDs solutions increased remarkably under irradiation, indicating the photothermal conversion ability. It took 20 min for the temperature to rise to 54.1 °C (CBO) and 75.7 °C (CBO-CDs), respectively. However, the pure H2O group only increased to 40.1 °C under the same conditions. The temperature increments for these samples can be clearly seen from the thermal imaging photos (Fig. 6E). Due to its narrow band gap, CBO exhibits exceptional light absorption capability, even in the NIR region, which endows it with photothermal ability [47,48]. CDs is a well-known photothermal agent, which further amplifies the photothermal behavior of CBO-CDs. It is widely accepted that the surface temperature of these photothermal agents significantly exceeds that of the surrounding solution. According to the Arrhenius equation, the temperature plays a positive role in reaction rate constant. The elevated temperature not only contributes to the photocatalytic reaction but also helps to damage the cell membranes of bacteria, as studied subsequently.

Considering the excellent photocatalytic activity, the antibacterial abilities of CBO-CDs against both Gram-negative (E. coli) and Gram-positive (S.aureus) bacteria were studied by using the commonly plate count method. As shown in Fig. 7 A and C, both CBO and CBO-CDs groups show the undisturbed E. coli growth and the bacterial survival rates are above 98.20 ± 3.90 % and 97.20 ± 4.67 % under dark conditions. Under irradiation, the bacterial colonies decrease depending on irradiation time as revealed in Fig. 7B. After 8 min of irradiation, the corresponding antibacterial rates increase to 30.26 ± 2.45 % for CBO and 96.88 ± 1.50 % for CBO-CDs, respectively (Fig. 7D). SEM was employed to examine the morphological alterations of bacteria during the photocatalytic process. As depicted in Fig. 7E–E coli in control group display typical rod-like shape with smooth surface, indicating their normal morphology. However, their surfaces become shriveled, rough, and even collapsed under irradiation, suggesting significant damage, particularly in CBO-CDs group. In addition, microorganisms inherently tend to self-organize into the structured, defensive and enduring biofilms, and these bacteria embedded within the biofilms exhibit markedly enhanced resistance to various antimicrobial interventions, leading to persistent and difficult-to-treat infections [49]. Here, the integrity of E. coli biofilm was investigated by utilizing crystal violet staining. From Fig. 7F, the conspicuous deep purple color in control and CBO-CDs group indicates the persistent biofilm structures. When the biofilms were treated with CBO-CDs + light, the notably decreased violet stain illuminates the severe disruption. Moreover, the respective optical density analysis (OD570) shows that CBO-CDs + light group reveals the highest efficacy in biofilm eradication (89.60 ± 2.10 %, Fig. 7G). Similarly, CBO-CDs also exhibit a significant antibacterial effect on S. aureus, as shown in Fig. 8 . After exposing to simulated sunlight for 25 min, the bacterial antibacterial ability of CBO and CBO-CDs decrease by 33.35 ± 1.32 % and 99.96 ± 1.41 %, respectively (Fig. 8A–D). As shown in SEM images, the smooth spheric morphology also turn to shrank and deformed seriously under irradiation (Fig. 8E). Additionally, CBO-CDs + light exhibits the great ability for the anti-biofilm capacity (90.70 ± 1.90 %, S. aureus). These data highlight that CBO-CDs + light really reveals the detrimental effects on bacterial integrity. As shown in Fig. S6, the inhibition rates of E. coli and S. aureus increases positively with time, which proves that CBO-CDs has high antibacterial efficiency depending on irradiation. Compared with other photocatalysts, CBO-CDs also reveal prominent antibacterial activity within 8 min with low concentration (130 μg/mL) derived from the novel nanotube structure and Z-scheme interface (Table S2). These data collectively demonstrate that CBO-CDs possess highly efficient photocatalytic antibacterial performance.

In order to assess the biosafety, CBO-CDs was cultured human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) for 24 h. As shown in Fig. 9 A, the morphologies of cells remain normal in comparable to that of control group. Additionally, the cells were stained using Calcein AM (green). After 24 h, the strong green fluorescence in all cells signifies the living cells. Moreover, cell viability was further assessed using MTT method (Fig. 9B). Even at a high concentration of 500 μg mL−1, the cell viability still remains above 98.65 ± 5.20 %. This result confirms the excellent biocompatibility of CBO-CDs, which is crucial for their potential practical applications.

Here, the photocatalytic disinfection of several real samples also was investigated. Firstly, the real lake water sample was taken out from Mengxi Lake (Harbin Normal University) for the examination as illustrated in Fig. 10 A. The CBO-CDs + light group exhibited significantly reduced bacterial colonies and a bacterial survival rate of 4.50 ± 0.51 %, as shown in Fig. 10B and C. In contrast, in the absence of photocatalyst or irradiation, no significant sterilization effect was observed and the viability remains above 98.84 ± 1.42 %. In order to further investigate the purification ability, methylene blue (MB) dye was added to lake water to simulate the complex water samples. As shown in 10D-F, the number of bacterial colonies reduces and the bluish MB solution also decolorized at the same time. After 30 min of irradiation, the bacteriostasis and dye decolorization rates increased to 97.65 ± 1.30 % and 99.91 ± 1.40 %, respectively. These results demonstrate the high efficiency of CBO-CDs + light in treating both bacterial and organic contamination, simultaneously.

Considering the great photocatalytic antibacterial activity, the other model also was investigated. Currently, an increasing number of individuals have opted to wear masks to mitigate the risk of airborne infections. The bacteriostasis for mask can contribute to the lower-risk of infection and the reuse. First, a blank mask and a mask + CBO-CDs were immersed in a medium containing 108 CFU/mL of E. coli. After incubation at 37 °C for 2 h, the masks were taken out and washed twice with PBS. Then, they were illuminated (simulated sun light) or placed in dark for 10 min. Finally, the masks were placed on solid medium and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. As shown in Fig. 10G, mask + CBO-CDs/light group reveals significant bacteriostatic ability while dense colonies can be found from control and mask + CBO-CDs/dark group. Moreover, the practical used-mask sample also was surveyed as shown in Fig. 10H and S7. As expected, negligible bacterial colonies were found from Mask + CBO-CDs/light group. Without treatment, the growth of bacterial colony increased exponentially. The above evidences indicate that CBO-CDs + light also can be applied for photocatalytic disinfection of masks.

Glutathione (GSH) is a crucial bacterial cell constituent, whose oxidation is always served as an oxidative stress indicator [50]. The negative control (without photocatalyst) exhibits minimal GSH oxidation (Fig. 11 A). Pure CBO + light displays 65.23 ± 3.65 % oxidation of GSH, and the oxidation extent in CBO-CDs + light group further increases to 89.45 ± 3.01 %, which is closer to the positive control group (97.45 ± 3.67 %). In the CBO-CDs + light group, the elevated oxidative stress can induce significant damage to the bacterial cell membrane, thereby playing a crucial role in promoting bacterial apoptosis. It is known that the direct destruction of cell wall enables the leakage of the cytoplasmic substances and ultimately leading to cell death. The leakage of intracellular proteins was determined through Bradford assay (Fig. 11B). The CBO + light group reveals protein leakage levels of 9.01 ± 1.45 μg mL−1 for E. coli and 8.52 ± 1.56 μg mL−1 S. aureus, respectively. And those further increase to 34.09 ± 1.67 μg mL−1 (E. coli) and 28.99 ± 1.88 μg mL−1 (S. aureus) in CBO-CDs + light. Moreover, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) is an enzyme, which plays a crucial role in energy production during anaerobic conditions. The amount of LDH release is another vital biomarker for cell injury and damage (Fig. 11C). The LDH release in CBO + light group reaches 58.35 ± 2.13 % (E. coli) and 51.45 ± 2.94 % (S. aureus). CBO-CDs + light can further increase LDH leakage (91.48 ± 2.45 %, E. coli and 88.78 ± 3.32 %, S. aureus). The significant protein and LDH leakage further confirm that the oxidative stress from CBO-CDs + light can destroy membrane permeability of bacteria, contributing to the release of all intracellular components and apoptosis (Fig. 11D).

In summary, CuBi2O4 nanomaterials were prepared by one-pot solvothermal method without any surfactant and template assistance. By varying the concentration of HNO3, CuBi2O4 nanoflowers were obtained, which were assembled by many nanotubes. And then, carbon dots were incorporated onto CuBi2O4 to form a Z-scheme heterostructure, which significantly enhances the photocatalytic ability (Fig. 12 ). 1) The loading of carbon dots increases the harvest of solar energy, which also enhances the photothermal ability to assist catalytic reaction. 2) The direct Z-scheme mechanism of CBO-CDs greatly improves the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and maintains the high redox ability for ROS generation. 3) The hierarchical structure is conducive to the adsorption of incoming species onto the surface, contributing to the subsequent catalytic reaction. Moreover, the nanotubes provide a short pathway for charge transfer and the vast interface, contributing to the efficient charge transfer. The above advantages facilitate the ROS production under simulated sun irradiation, which can destroy cell membranes and degrade cytogenetic constituents, inducing antibacterial effect. Under simulated sun irradiation, the photocatalytic antibacterial rate can reach 96.88 ± 1.50 % (E. coli)/99.96 ± 1.41 % (S.aureus) and above 91.70 ± 2.54 % of biofilm can be eliminated. The complex practical water sample also was investigated to reveal great bacteriostatic (97.65 ± 1.30 %) and dye degradation (99.91 ± 1.4 0 %) ability at the same time. In addition, CBO-CDs nanocomposite can be used for self-degermed mask. The novel biocompatibility associated with notable photocatalytic capacity, grants the nanocomposite great promise for sterilization applications in diverse practical settings.
